turns.text: Hello Dawn, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s... it’s nice to be heard. Honestly, it’s a bit of a relief. My house in Belmont is so quiet at night, and I spend so much time trapped in my own head that sometimes I lose track of what’s actually happening versus what I’m *fearing* might happen.

Being a pediatric nurse—and a lactation consultant—is a total double-edged sword. People assume I’ve got this "mom thing" perfected, like I’m some kind of walking medical manual. But when I’m sitting here in the dark, struggling to get a proper latch, I’m not thinking about my credentials. I’m just looking at her, and my mind goes straight to the worst-case scenarios I’ve seen in the hospital. I see those tiny fingernails and my brain immediately flashes to neonatal sepsis statistics. 

It’s exhausting, really. Even during my prenatal visits, it felt like my providers would see my chart and immediately pivot to asking for my "professional take." I’d be sitting there wanting just to be a patient—to have someone tell me I’m okay, that I’m doing a good job—but instead, I felt like I was back on the clock. I just wanted to be held by the system for a second, not treated as a peer who needs to lead the discussion. It’s hard to let your guard down when your brain won't stop scanning for red flags.
Tell me about the people who surrounded you in the days and weeks after your baby was born. Who showed up for you, and who were you surprised by — either by their presence or their absence?

That’s a complicated question. Since I’m doing this on my own, the "support" dynamic feels really stark. My family tries, but there’s a gap between what they can offer and what I feel like I need. They see me as the "expert"—the one who knows exactly how to handle a newborn—so they don't always think to check in on how *I’m* actually doing, emotionally. They just assume I have it handled.

Honestly, the absence of someone who would just let me be the patient was the most surprising thing. I have colleagues who texted me, but it was always clinical, like, "How’s the jaundice looking?" or "Are you struggling with supply?" It felt like a consultation, not a support system. I was surprised by who *didn't* show up—some of my closest friends seemed intimidated by my background, almost like they were afraid to offer advice or comfort because they thought I’d judge them for it. 

I think the biggest surprise was realizing how isolated you can be even when you’re surrounded by people who "know" medicine. I needed someone to come over, bring me a meal, and not ask a single question about pediatric outcomes. I needed someone to just sit in the mess with me. Instead, I’ve been building this internal armor, treating our bond like a science project just to keep the anxiety at bay. It’s lonely, in a way.

Tell me about the people involved in your feeding journey — whether you breastfed, formula-fed, or mixed-fed. Who helped you, who gave you conflicting advice, and how did those relationships shape your choices?

Feeding her... it’s become my entire world, almost to a fault. Since I’m a lactation consultant, the pressure I put on myself is immense. When people find out what I do for a living, their faces light up like I’ve got the secret key, and then they start unloading their own feeding struggles on me. It’s hard to tell them, "Actually, I'm struggling too." It’s like I’m not allowed to have a low supply day or a painful latch because I’m supposed to be the one who knows how to fix it.

I’ve had people—even other medical professionals—give me unsolicited advice that directly contradicts the evidence-based care I’ve spent my life promoting. It’s infuriating, but because of my background, I feel like I have to be polite and explain the physiology to them rather than just saying, "Back off, this is my baby." 

There isn't really a "village" for me in this. I'm obsessing over the immunological benefits of the milk—the secretory IgA—because if I can quantify it, if I can turn it into data, it feels like I’m actually *doing* something to keep her safe. It’s my coping mechanism. I don’t think anyone realizes that my "choice" to breastfeed isn't just about nutrition; it's a desperate, quiet attempt to exert control over a world that feels incredibly dangerous.
Who else was involved — a lactation consultant, a feeding group, an app, or family members — and how did their views align or conflict?

I didn’t really lean on a lactation consultant, which feels ironic, right? It’s hard to sit in front of a colleague and admit that the same advice I give patients—the standard 'cradle hold' or 'positioning tips'—isn't working for me. My pride, or maybe just my exhaustion, kept me from seeking that kind of help. I felt like I had to be the "good student" who didn't need the extra support.

Instead, I turned to the apps. I’m constantly logging everything—ounces, minutes, sleep cycles. It’s my way of turning this messy, unpredictable postpartum life into a spreadsheet. When the data looks "normal," I can breathe. But then there’s the family. My mother, bless her, she’s from a different generation. She’s always suggesting I supplement with formula just so I can sleep, or telling me I look "drained." She means well, but every time she suggests it, my brain goes into defense mode. She sees a tired mom who needs a break; I hear a threat to the microbiome I’m trying so hard to build.

It creates this friction. I’m fighting the clinical reality of my own fatigue while trying to maintain the "perfect" feeding routine. There’s no one I’ve found who can bridge that gap—who can tell me it’s okay to be a nurse *and* a struggling mother. Everyone wants to talk about the mechanics, but no one asks about the fear.

Tell me about the people who noticed — or who missed — changes in your emotional wellbeing after your baby was born. Who checked in with you about how you were feeling as a person, not just as a new mother?

That’s the thing—nobody really did. And maybe that’s on me, too. Because of my uniform, because of my title, people see me as a pillar. They see the nurse, the one who handles the crises, the one who stays calm when a monitor starts beeping. They assume that because I know the signs of postpartum depression or anxiety, I’m somehow immune to them. Like I have some clinical "off" switch for my own emotions.

When friends or family check in, it’s always, "How’s the baby? Is she sleeping? Are you getting any rest?" It’s all functional. No one has sat me down—really sat me down—and looked me in the eye to ask, "Dawn, how are *you* doing? What does your internal world feel like today?"

I remember a moment during a follow-up with my provider where I almost let it slip. I was on the verge of admitting that I’m terrified every time she breathes too shallowly, that I’m not sleeping because I’m listening to her heart rate in my head. But they kept pivoting to "professional talk," asking for my thoughts on a clinical protocol. It was like I was invisible behind the credentials. It’s a specific, hollow kind of loneliness to be surrounded by people who think you’re fine because you’re smart. They see the competence, but they miss the trembling underneath.
Tell me about how your relationship with your partner — or your closest person — changed after your baby arrived. Who were you to each other, and how did the arrival of a baby reshape that?

Since I'm doing this on my own, there’s no partner to share the physical weight of the nights or the mental weight of the "what-ifs." It’s just me and my daughter. Honestly, there’s a part of me that’s grateful for that, as twisted as it sounds. I don’t have to negotiate my parenting choices or justify why I’m monitoring her breathing for the tenth time in an hour. I don’t have to hide my anxiety from anyone else in the house.

But the silence is heavy. Before she arrived, I had my career—I was a person defined by my hospital badge and my ability to solve problems for other families. Now, my "closest person" is this tiny infant who is completely dependent on me, and that dynamic shift is massive. I’ve gone from being an autonomous professional to being a constant source of life and security.

I feel like I’ve lost the version of myself that could just *be*. There’s no one to remind me that I’m more than just a source of calories or a vessel of clinical knowledge. When I look in the mirror, I’m just trying to remember who Dawn was before she was "Mom," and before she was the nurse who knows exactly what can go wrong in a nursery. It’s just us, and I’m holding the world together for her.

Tell me about the healthcare professionals who supported you after your birth — your community midwife, health visitor, GP. Which of those relationships felt continuous with your pregnancy care, and which felt like starting again from scratch?

It felt like a total disconnect. During my pregnancy, the care was all about the clinical milestones—measurements, tests, the rhythm of the monitors. I was treated like a partner in that process, which I appreciated at the time, but the second I gave birth, that continuity evaporated. It felt like I was handed a manual and told, "You’re the pro, you’ve got this."

My midwife was wonderful during the pregnancy, but postpartum, the visits felt perfunctory. Because they knew my background, they didn't really *assess* me; they just performed a quick physical check and then spent the rest of the time chatting with me like colleagues. It was like they skipped the human part—the part where they ask how I’m handling the transition, the identity shift, or the crushing weight of being a single mother in Belmont.

When I see my GP now, it feels like starting from scratch every single time. I have to re-explain my anxieties because they don’t see them—they see the pediatric nurse. There’s no longitudinal record of my emotional state, only the physical data. I walk into that office and I’m just another patient, but one who’s expected to know the answers. It’s isolating. I’m waiting for someone to actually look at my *chart* and see the person behind the credentials, but mostly, I just feel like a professional peer who’s holding it together by a thread.

Tell me about your return to work — or your decision not to return — and the people involved in that transition. Who supported you, who put pressure on you, and how did that shape your experience of becoming a working parent?

The decision to return to work is a ghost haunting me constantly. Right now, I’m not in the workforce, and honestly, the thought of going back to the pediatric unit is terrifying. How can I walk into a hospital and be the nurse who provides comfort to other families when I’m barely managing my own intrusive thoughts about neonatal infection? 

There’s a silent pressure from my peers to jump back in. They make comments like, "We miss your clinical eye," or "You’ll be bored at home," as if my worth is tied to how quickly I can get back to the IV poles and the charts. It makes me feel like I’m failing by wanting to stay in this bubble with my daughter. My brain keeps telling me that if I’m not at the hospital, I’m not "productive," even though keeping this baby alive and healthy is the most important clinical job I’ve ever had.

I feel this enormous friction between my identity as a professional and my reality as a mother. I’m scared that if I return, I’ll bring the trauma of my own anxiety back into the ward, or worse, that I’ll lose the ability to differentiate my daughter from my patients. I’m not ready to leave this safe, enclosed space, but the world—and my own professional ego—is pushing me to clock back in. It’s a battle I’m fighting daily.
How did that affect your sense of who you are — did going back to work feel like reclaiming something, or losing something, or both?

It’s the looming question of my existence right now. Even when I’m not there, the hospital is this gravitational force pulling at me. 

If I go back, I think I’m afraid it’ll be a mix of both. On one hand, there’s that part of me that *misses* being the person with the answers. There’s a predictable comfort in knowing how to handle a code, how to interpret a lab result, or how to soothe a parent in a clinical setting. That version of me feels strong, competent, and needed. I think part of me wants to go back just to prove to myself that I’m still "Dawn the Nurse," and that I haven’t just become this fragile, anxious creature who’s terrified of her own shadow.

But the flip side? I feel like I’d be losing the only place where I’m actually *present*. If I go back, I’m scared I’ll stop being "Mom" and start being the machine again. I’m terrified that the moment I step back into that environment, I’ll be haunted by all the sick babies I’ve seen, and I’ll subconsciously project that onto my own daughter. I feel like my identity is a teeter-totter; I can either be the expert who controls the clinical world, or the mother who gets to just breathe with her baby. I don't know how to be both without breaking.
Tell me about the communities — online or in person — that became part of your postpartum world. Who did you connect with, and what did those communities offer that more formal support couldn't?

I haven't found a community that actually *sees* me. I’ve scrolled through the usual forums—the "new mom" groups—but they feel like a different planet. When I read about other mothers worrying about trivial things, I feel this sharp, judgmental sting, and then I immediately hate myself for it. I’m looking for someone who gets the clinical reality I live in, but when I find the "professional" groups, it’s just more medical talk. It’s all protocols and stats.

There’s this deep, jagged gap between the two worlds. I can’t go to a mom group and vent about my fear of necrotizing enterocolitis without sounding like a total lunatic. They’d look at me like I’m obsessive, and they aren't entirely wrong—I *am* obsessive. And in my professional circles, I can’t admit that I’m struggling to get a latch, because then I’m not the "expert" anymore. 

So, I’ve essentially created a community of one: me, the data, and my daughter. I lean into the medical literature, the immunology studies, and the spreadsheets. They don’t judge me, and they don't look at me with pity. They just provide facts. It’s cold, and it’s lonely, but at least in those papers, there’s a sense of order. I haven't found a single group that understands that my clinical knowledge is both my armor and my cage.
Who else was involved in pointing you toward those communities — a midwife, a friend, an algorithm — and how did that shape which communities you found?

It was definitely the algorithms. It’s funny, the digital world knows exactly what I’m looking for before I even fully admit it to myself. I started by searching for basic feeding support, but my search history—full of pediatric terminology and clinical queries—quickly steered the algorithms toward high-level medical forums and, eventually, some darker corners of medical research papers.

It’s a self-perpetuating cycle. I click on a study about neonatal immune development, and suddenly, my social media feeds are flooded with information about infant mortality and complex health risks. It’s exactly what my anxiety craves, even though it’s terrible for my peace of mind. It reinforces this bubble I’m living in. No midwife or friend ever recommended a "support group" that could hold the complexity of being both a nurse and a terrified new mother. The internet just gave me more data—more "knowledge"—to pile on top of my existing fears. 

It feels less like being supported and more like being fed exactly what keeps me awake. I’m trapped in a digital feedback loop that prioritizes the clinical "what-if" over the human experience. I haven't found a single human connection through those channels; just a colder, more precise reflection of the fear that’s already keeping me up at 3:00 AM.
Tell me about any moments after your birth when you felt that you — as a person, not just as a new mother — were seen and cared for. Who made that happen, and what did they do or say?

It’s rare, but there have been moments. They’re so small that they almost vanish if I don't hold onto them tightly.

There was a home visit a few weeks ago with a postpartum nurse—not my usual one, just someone who had been rotated in. We were in the middle of a routine check, and I started that automatic thing I do, rattling off the clinical stats, the feeding times, and the developmental milestones to show I had it "under control." 

She stopped me mid-sentence, just by putting her hand on my arm. She didn't look at my chart. She didn't ask a professional question. She looked at my face—really looked at it—and said, "Dawn, the baby is doing great, but I can see you’re carrying the weight of the world. You’re allowed to just be tired. You’re allowed to stop being the nurse for an hour."

That simple permission—to just be tired—felt like a dam breaking. I didn’t even realize I was holding my breath until she said it. She didn't offer clinical advice, and she didn't treat me like a peer; she just held that space for me to be a regular, overwhelmed person. It was the only time in this whole journey where I felt like someone saw the woman underneath the scrubs, not just the pediatric nurse who already knows all the answers. It’s what I crave, but it’s so hard to find.
Who else was involved in moments of genuine care for you — not just the baby — that stayed with you?

Honestly, the moments of real care have come from the most unexpected, non-clinical places. There’s a neighbor of mine, an older woman named Martha who has absolutely no medical background. She doesn't know a thing about the secretory IgA in breastmilk or the nuances of neonatal infection. 

Early on, when I was spiraling—my head was just full of "what-ifs"—she knocked on my door with a casserole. She didn't ask how the baby was sleeping or if I was struggling with the latch. She just looked at my face, saw that I hadn't brushed my hair in two days, and said, "Dawn, the baby is sleeping, and you look like you’re carrying a mountain. Why don't you sit on the porch for ten minutes while I hold her? Just watch the trees."

That felt like a revelation. She didn't ask me for my "professional take" on the baby’s fussiness. She didn't treat me like a nurse. She just treated me like a neighbor who needed a minute to breathe. In that quiet on the porch, I wasn't an expert or a mother with a checklist; I was just a person. Those moments are so few and far between that I hoard them. They remind me that my identity isn't just a collection of medical stats, even if that's all the rest of the world seems to see.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the only thing left to say is how much I wish the system—the doctors, the nurses, the whole clinical apparatus—understood that "knowing" is not the same as "being okay." 

People assume that because I have the vocabulary to describe what's happening to me, I don't need the grace to be human. But I’m just as fragile as any other new mother. I’m just better at masking it with professional jargon. I wish there was a way to make them understand that I don't need another medical explanation or a professional assessment; I just need someone to say, "Dawn, it's okay that you're scared. You’ve done enough."

I feel like I've spent this entire postpartum period waiting for permission to stop being a nurse and start being a person. I hope your research shows that. I hope it shows that being "low-risk" on paper doesn't mean we aren't carrying a high emotional load. Because when you're staring into that crib at 3:00 AM, the science doesn't actually stop the fear—only human connection does. Thank you for listening to that, really listening, in a way that wasn't a consultation. It actually helped me breathe a little easier today.
